paper_title: Swift Sampling: Selecting Temporal Surprises via Taylor Series

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: While most frames in long-form video are redundant, the critical information resides in temporal surprises: moments where the actual visual features deviate from their predicted evolution. Inspired by the human brain's predictive coding, we introduce Swift Sampling, an elegant, training-free frame selection algorithm that automatically identifies high-information moments in a video. Specifically, we model a video as a differentiable trajectory in the visual latent space and compute the velocity and acceleration of its features. Then, we apply Taylor expansion to project the expected path of subsequent frames. Frames that diverge sharply from this predicted manifold are identified as temporally surprising frames and selected for sampling. Unlike prior training-free methods that rely on auxiliary networks or video-specific hyperparameter tuning, Swift Sampling is incredibly lightweight, adding only 0.02x additional computational cost over baseline making it 30x cheaper overhead than leading baselines. Across three long-video question answering benchmarks and 10 different downstream tasks, Swift Sampling outperforms uniform sampling and prior query-agnostic baselines. It is especially powerful for long videos with limited frame budgets improving accuracy by up to +12.5 points.

sections.content: Dahye Kim1 Bhuvan Sachdeva2 Karan Uppal2 Naman Gupta2 Vineeth N. Balasubramanian2 Deepti Ghadiyaram 1Boston University 2Microsoft Research India Figure 1: Swift Sampling efficiently identifies temporal surprises in videos by measuring how much frame deviates from the trajectory predicted by its preceding context. Using Taylor expansion of visual features, we select frames with the largest residuals within their temporal neighborhood as keyframes. Top: Temporal surprise captured using Taylor residual over time. Bottom: input frames and frames selected by Uniform sampling (orange), Cosine Uniqueness [1] (yellow), and our method (green). Swift Sampling captures the videos most informative frames with 30 less overhead than Cosine Uniqueness, while delivering +12.5% improvement on VQA tasks on long videos with tight frame budgets.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: While most frames in long-form video are redundant, the critical information resides in temporal surprises: moments where the actual visual features deviate from their predicted evolution. Inspired by the human brains predictive coding, we introduce Swift Sampling, an elegant, training-free frame selection algorithm that automatically identifies high-information moments in video. Specifically, we model video as differentiable trajectory in the visual latent space and compute the velocity and acceleration of its features. Then, we apply Taylor expansion to project the expected path of subsequent frames. Frames that diverge sharply from this predicted manifold are identified as temporally surprising frames and selected for sampling. Unlike prior training-free methods that rely on auxiliary networks or video-specific hyperparameter tuning, Swift Sampling is incredibly lightweight, adding only 0.02 additional computational cost over baseline making it 30 cheaper overhead than leading baselines. Across three long-video question answering benchmarks and 10 different downstream tasks, Swift Sampling outperforms uniform sampling and prior query-agnostic baselines. It is especially powerful for long videos with limited frame budgets improving accuracy by up to +12.5 points. *Equal contribution. Preprint.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: How does the human brain process the simple sight of polar bear walking through the snow? Rather than exhaustively processing the continuous visual stream, our visual system is known to operate and revise as predictive engine: it anticipates future states and revises its internal model by calculating the residual errors between its prediction and reality [2, 3]. As result, our visual systems computational budget is not wasted on the predictable trajectory of the bear, but is instead reserved for temporal surprises, such as the sudden appearance of seal. This biological principle inspired seminal video compression [4] algorithms and motivates the present work. Long-form video is dominated by temporal redundancy: frames evolve slowly and predictably for extended stretches, punctuated by sparse but informative transitions. Yet, most Video Large Language Models (VLMs) still rely on uniform sampling to reduce video to fixed frame budget [5, 6, 7], not considering temporal structure and treating redundant frames identically to pivotal ones. Alternative approaches, such as using optical flow [8] and pairwise frame-similarity methods [1, 9], partially address this, but have their own limitations. First, they require separate, often external, vision encoder to extract per-frame representations [8, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15], nearly doubling the inference cost. Second, they require careful, video-specific hyperparameter tuning to define what constitutes significant change. The computational overhead negates the efficiency gains they offer, and hyperparameter sensitivity can adversely affect downstream task performance. Our method is based on simple observation: long-form video consists of vast, highly predictable intervals interjected with sparse temporal surprises. We ask: can we leverage the biologically elegant predictive coding principle to identify these temporal surprises, where frames content diverges from its expected path, without auxiliary models or manual tuning? To this end, we propose Swift Sampling, framework that treats the visual latent features of adjacent video frames as points lying on locally smooth trajectory  (Fig. 2)  . This makes it amenable to apply polynomial approximation via Taylor series using higher order derivatives. Given the feature vectors of the frames preceding the current frame t, we construct Taylor predictor that captures velocity (first order), acceleration (second order), and jerk (third order) of the feature trajectory. The Taylor residual the ℓ2 distance between the predicted and the observed feature serves as principled, per-frame informativeness score. small residual indicates predictable, redundant frame (e.g., bears rhythmic walk), while large residual signals temporal surprise, i.e., moment of genuinely new information (e.g., the sudden emergence of seal out of ice). For given frame budget K, we select the local maxima of the residual sequence, prioritizing the most surprising frame within each local temporal context  (Fig. 1)  . The sampling rate scales naturally with the video complexity making our approach hyperparameter-light. Crucially, we compute these residuals directly from the intermediate representations of the VLMs vision encoder that must be computed anyway during the forward pass. Our results highlight that the temporal surprise detection based on Taylor expansion is robust enough to serve as drop-in replacement for expensive previous methods, bridging the gap between low-level temporal motion and high-level LLM reasoning. Below, we summarize our contributions: We propose Swift Sampling, training-free frame selection algorithm that operationalizes predictive coding by scoring frames via their Taylor series residual in the VLMs latent space, with no auxiliary model or any videospecific tuning making it hyperparameter-light and efficient. Swift Sampling achieves state-of-the-art performance over uniform sampling and several prior training-free methods across different VLM backbones on video question answering, token compression, and over ten other reasoning tasks across diverse video lengths. We provide systematic analysis of the design choices of Swift Sampling, yielding critical insights into the relationship between latent temporal dynamics and frame selection. Figure 2: Each frame is represented on the latent feature trajectory, where we apply Taylor expansion over preceding frames to predict the next frame feature. The residual between the prediction and the actual feature measures how much the trajectory deviates from smooth continuation. Frames with large residuals correspond to temporal surprises, e.g., seal suddenly emerging from the ice, which Swift Sampling effectively captures.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Video large language models and long video understanding. Video large language models have achieved impressive results on short-form video understanding [5, 6, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23], but processing long videos remains challenging due to the large number of input frames. To better handle long-form inputs, prior works improve temporal modeling [5, 17, 19], multimodal fusion [7, 16, 18, 21], and multi-scale encoding [6, 20, 22, 23, 24, 25]; others explicitly target long videos through context-length extension [25, 26, 27], temporal token compression [21, 28, 29], or KV-cache sparsification [30]. Despite these advances, most approaches still rely on uniform sampling to reduce raw videos to fixed number of frames, overlooking redundancy among sampled frames. We focus on this preprocessing stage, selecting non-redundant frames to make better use of the limited frame budget, which is orthogonal and complementary to these model-level improvements. Frame selection for long video understanding. Frame selection methods for long-video understanding have been actively explored along two directions: training-based and training-free approaches. Training-based methods learn to select frames through end-to-end optimization with downstream task losses [31, 32], frame-candidate ranking [33], pseudo-label supervision from visionlanguage models [34], reinforcement or self-learning [35, 36, 37, 38], and supervised keyframe annotations [39, 40]. Although effective, these methods often require additional training or adaptation for each VLM [32, 33, 34], which is expensive and limits practical deployment. To avoid this limitation, training-free frame selection methods have been preferred. Query-aware methods have been heavily explored [41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 46, 47, 48, 49], which select frames based on text-visual similarity with the language query. Query-agnostic methods [50] select frames solely from visual features without access to the query. However, both categories typically require encoding all candidate frames with separate vision encoder to compute frame-level representations, which can nearly double inference cost. By contrast, Swift Sampling avoids the need for an auxiliary model by leveraging the VLMs own vision encoder, thereby incurring negligible computational overhead. Tokenization-based approaches such as ElasticTok [51], EVATok [52], AdapTok [53], and InfoTok [54], dynamically adjust the number of tokens according to video content complexity. Similarly, methods such as ToMe [55] and PruneVid [56] focus on efficiency by merging spatially or temporally redundant tokens. In contrast, Swift Sampling first identifies the most informative frames to retain prior to tokenization. By filtering redundant frames at the input level, Swift Sampling offers complementary layer of efficiency that can be combined with token-level compression strategies. Taylor series for video understanding. The Taylor series approximates function at given point using its derivatives, decomposing local behavior into zeroth-order (value), first-order (velocity), second-order (acceleration), and higher-order terms. This predictive structure has been used in video understanding and generation. Taylor Video [57] sums higher-order Taylor residuals into dedicated motion representation that replaces or complements RGB frames as input to action classifiers; ViDiDi [58] uses temporal derivatives as additional views for self-supervised video representation learning. More recently, TaylorSeer [59] and SCOPE [60] use Taylor prediction to estimate future features across diffusion denoising steps, skipping recomputation when the prediction is reliable. These works use Taylor terms primarily to construct new representations or accelerate generation. In contrast, we use the magnitude of the Taylor residual as frame-level informativeness score: frames whose features deviate strongly from their predicted trajectory are treated as informative and selected as keyframes. While Taylor expansions have been used before, we are not aware of any prior works that use them as training-free, query-agnostic frame selector for very long videos.

sections.title: 3 Swift Sampling: Our Approach
sections.content: Given video with frames and target budget of , our objective is to select the most informative frames for downstream video model. To achieve this, we propose Swift Sampling, selection strategy grounded in the Taylor series expansion of latent visual features. Sec. 3.1 introduces the Taylor predictor for latent feature sequences, and Sec. 3.2 formalizes the Taylor residual as principled informativeness score and presents the full selection algorithm.

sections.title: 3.1 Background: Taylor Series Expansion for Sequence Prediction
sections.content: Let be smooth scalar-valued function of time, let t0 denote the current timestep and let x(n)(t0) denote the n-th derivative of at t0. The Taylor series predicts at future time t0 + from higher order derivatives of x, defined as follows: x(t0 + t) = (cid:88) n=0 tn n! x(n)(t0) = x(t0) + x(1)(t0) + 2! x(2)(t0) + . (1) In practice, is observed only at discrete timesteps, so derivatives must be approximated by backward finite differences [61]. The first-order derivative is approximated as the difference between the two most recent samples, x(1)(t0) x(t0) x(t0 t) , and the second-order derivative as the difference of two consecutive first-order differences, x(2)(t0) x(1)(t0) x(1)(t0 t) = x(t0) 2x(t0 t) + x(t0 2t) t2 . (2) (3) In general, the n-th order approximation is linear combination of current and preceding frames (thus + 1 total frames). This is derived by applying the difference operator times to the sequence x(t0), x(t0 t), . . . , x(t0 nt), with weights determined by binomial coefficients. x(n)(t0) 1 tn (cid:32) (cid:88) (1)k k=0 (cid:33) x(t0 t). (4) Substituting these estimates into Eq. (1) yields closed-form linear combination of preceding samples, enabling efficient prediction of x(t0 + t) directly from observations.

sections.title: 3.2 Taylor Residual as an Informativeness Signal
sections.content: Using the Taylor residual. Let ft Rd denote the visual feature vector extracted from the video frame at time and let (n) t1 denote the n-th order derivative of the visual feature trajectory at time 1. natural criterion for frame informativeness under fixed budget is temporal surprise: frame (feature) ft is informative if its content is not predictable from the preceding context f1, . . . , ft1. Predictive coding theory formalizes this intuition by equating informativeness with prediction error, i.e., the discrepancy between the observed signal and the best prediction derived from prior context [2]. For latent feature trajectory that evolves smoothly in time, the natural local predictor is the Taylor expansion ˆft = ft1 + t1 (t)2 + , which extrapolates the trajectory under the assumption of locally polynomial dynamics. t1 + 1 2 As noted in Eq. 4, the n-th order derivative can be approximated using backward finite-differences from the sequence of preceding features, i.e., {ft1, . . . , ft1n}. Assuming uniform temporal spacing (t = 1) and truncating Eq. (1) at order , following prior works [61, 62, 63, 59], we define the Taylor predictor of ft based on its + 1 predecessors as: ˆf (N ) = ft1 + (1) t1 + 1 2! (2) t1 + + 1 ! (N ) Now, temporal surprise or Taylor residual at frame is the magnitude of the prediction error: (cid:13) (cid:13)ft ˆf (N ) (cid:13) rt = . (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13)2 (5) (6) While the Taylor predictor captures the trajectorys local kinematic structure such as velocity, acceleration, jerk, etc., rt isolates the surprise, the component of ft not explained by smooth extrapolation. Concretely, frames with large residual rt deviate sharply from the predicted trajectory, indicating high information content relative to their temporal context. Conversely, frames with small rt closely adhere to the predicted path and are considered redundant. Consequently, the Taylor residual sequence {rt} provides principled, per-frame informativeness signal across the candidate pool. Information-theoretic motivation: From statistical perspective, under an isotropic Gaussian model for innovation (novel information) ft = ˆf (N ) + ϵt, ϵt (0, σ2I), following [64, Ch. 8, 4 Figure 3: Qualitative comparison of frame selection on sample video from VideoMME dataset, given budget of 8 frames out of 128. The correct answer (a seal at the breathing hole) requires temporal coverage of multiple events. Uniform sampling is redundant, capturing the polar bear while missing the seal entirely. Cosine Uniqueness [1] favors visual outliers like title cards and underwater shots that are task irrelevant and fail to provide relevant information. By contrast, Swift Sampling captures the temporal surprise of the seals appearance thus providing critical evidence for correct reasoning. Thm 8.4.1], the Shannon self-information (surprise) of frame (feature) ft given its context can be written as: log p(cid:0)ft f1, . . . , ft1 (cid:1) = 1 2σ2 r2 + const , (7) which is monotonically increasing in the Taylor-residual magnitude rt. While this Gaussian model is an idealization (we do not claim that the vision encoders projections are Gaussian), it motivates our use of Taylor residual as tractable surrogate for informativeness. We note that this interpretation is consistent with classical filtering formulations, where larger innovations induce larger posterior corrections (e.g., [65]). Local Maxima Selection: key subtlety is that rt is computed relative to its predecessors, so its absolute scale depends on the local dynamics of the trajectory: slow, uniform scene yields consistently low residuals, while fast-moving segment produces uniformly high values. Consequently, selecting the global top-K residuals would concentrate all keyframes within few high-motion bursts, leaving subtler but critical events entirely unrepresented. We therefore select the local maxima of {rt}, identifying the most surprising frame within each local temporal context, regardless of absolute magnitude. Formally, let < denote the number of detected local maxima, which vary with the video content. Formally, we define the set of local maxima = {p1, . . . , pM }, indexed in increasing temporal order as: = (cid:8) : ri > ri1 and ri > ri+1 (cid:9). From this candidate set P, we select the elements with the largest residuals to serve as the final frames. In cases where the video is highly static (M < K), the remaining slots are filled using the highest-residual frames from the pool of non-maxima, {1, . . . , } P. We demonstrate in Sec. 4 that this hierarchical selection strategy prioritizes the most significant surprises relative to their immediate context.

sections.title: 4.1 Experimental Settings
sections.content: Benchmarks. We evaluate on three well-known long video benchmarks: Video-MME [70], MLVU [71], and LongVideoBench (LVB) [72]. Each benchmark focuses specifically on Visual Question Answering (VQA) as the primary downstream reasoning task. To measure how frame selection quality scales with video duration, we report accuracy across various temporal subsets, ranging from short clips to videos exceeding 30 minutes. Baselines. We compare Swift Sampling against training-based supervised methods [33, 34, 39] designed specifically for frame selection, training-free query-aware methods (marked as in Table 1), that utilize the input question to identify relevant frames during inference [41], training-free query-agnostic methods (marked as in Table 1) that select frames based purely on video content, including MaxInfo [50] and the following baselines: 1) Cosine Uniqueness [1], which selects the top-K most unique frames based on inter-frame cosine similarity; 2) Frame Difference, which selects the top-K frames with the largest adjacentframe feature differences; 3) I-Frame and 4) P-Frame, which select the top-K frames ranked by I-frame or P-frame packet sizes from the video codec, respectively; 5) Optical Table 1: VQA accuracy across different video durations on Video-MME, LongVideoBench (LVB), and MLVU benchmark. The Query-agnostic column indicates if the method selects frames without using the query (: query-agnostic, : queryaware). The FLOPs column reports inference cost relative to uniform sampling on the same backbone (1.00 = no overhead beyond the base VLMs forward pass). Within each (backbone, query-type) block: bold = best, underline = second best. Details in the appendix. Implementation details. We use two representative VLM backbones for our experiments: LLaVAOneVision [7] and LLaVA-Video [5]. Given video, we uniformly sample 128 candidate frames and select = 32 frames for downstream tasks. We extract frame representations from the key projections of the vision encoders first transformer layer (ℓ = 0) to compute the Taylor residuals. The spatial tokens are mean-pooled to obtain the per-frame feature ft used in Eq. 6. Throughout all experiments, we fix the Taylor expansion order to = 3. For all baselines, we strictly adhere to their publicly available implementations. More details in Appendix.

sections.title: 4.2 Main Results
sections.content: Results are summarized in Tables 1 and 2. Swift Sampling brings consistent gains over uniform sampling across all two backbones, with particularly strong improvements on long-duration videos. Using LLaVA-OneVision as backbone, on the LVB dataset, the overall accuracy improves from 55.3 to 57.9 (+2.6); on MLVU dataset, the overall jumps from 64.7 to 65.6 (+0.9). The gains are more pronounced on longer videos: +6.8 points on LVB videos longer than 20 minutes (47.5 54.3), +6.4 points on LVB videos longer than 10 minutes (45.2 51.6), and +4.2 points on MLVU videos longer than 30 minutes (50.0 54.2). On LLaVA-Video, we observe similar trends: MLVU overall improves from 64.2 to 67.2 (+3.0), and LVB videos longer than 20 minutes from 53.3 to 56.2 (+2.9). As shown in Fig. 3, these improvements stem from our methods ability to capture pivotal surprise events that standard baselines overlook. On broader comparison with query-agnostic baselines ( in Table 1 and the entirety of Table 2), our method remains highly competitive while operating at negligible 1.02 inference cost. This efficiency stems from reusing the target Video LLMs existing vision encoder specifically the first few layers to compute frame representations. This adds only 0.02 to the total inference cost compared to the vanilla model. By contrast, existing training-free methods [1, 41, 69, 50] require 6 Figure 4: Qualitative comparison of frame selection on sample video from the Video-MME dataset, given budget to select 8 frames out of 128. Answering the question requires identifying the temporal order of several visually similar but semantically distinct painting events: establishing the background, drawing the water-lily pads, adding flowers, and increasing texture. Uniform sampling captures the background and water-lily pads but misses the frames showing the addition of flowers and texture, leading to an incorrect answer. Cosine Uniqueness [1] over-selects visually salient but task-irrelevant frames, such as title cards and end screens, which is especially harmful under limited frame budget. Swift Sampling focuses on temporally informative changes in the painting progression, captures key intermediate stages and enables correct temporal reasoning. encoding all candidate frames through separate, often external vision encoder [13, 14, 37, 73], increasing inference cost to approximately 1.8. Notice that the advantage of Swift Sampling is most pronounced in the long-video regime: on LVB videos longer than 20 minutes, we outperform the strongest baseline (MaxInfo at 50.8) by +3.5 points (54.3); on MLVU videos longer than 30 minutes, we outperform the strongest baselines (Iframe and Optical Flow, both at 52.1) by +2.1 points (54.2). Combining with AKS. Swift Sampling is plug-and-play framework that can be combined with other query-aware () frame selection methods such as AKS [41], that score candidate frames against the posed question. In this context, Swift Sampling serves as high-speed pre-filter, narrowing the candidate pool before more computationally expensive query-based scoring takes place. As shown in Table 1, pre-filtering from 128 to 96 candidates (AKS+Swift Sampling) reduces the selection cost of AKS from 1.53 to 1.43. Crucially, this efficiency does not come at the expense of performance; rather, it improves overall accuracy across all three benchmarks, including gains of +0.8 points on MLVU and +0.6 points on LVB. Additionally, by initiating the process with larger pool of 256 frames and pre-filtering to 128, we outperform the standard AKS (256 32) by +2.1% on Video-MME and +0.5% on MLVU. Remarkably, this performance boost is achieved while simultaneously reducing the inference costs from 2.06 to 1.59. In summary, Swift Sampling is robust, architecture-agnostic technique that selects pivotal frames for long-form video processing. In the following sections, we show the utility of Swift Sampling in diverse video understanding tasks.

sections.title: 4.3 Application: Token Compression
sections.content: Token compression seeks to reduce visual token counts while preserving core video content. We integrate Swift Sampling into UniComp [1], the current state-of-the-art in this domain, by replacing its default uniform frame selection (indicated as +Swift Sampling (Ours), 128 32 in Table 3). Our intuition is that providing UniComp with more informative initial frames will yield superior results within the same frame budget. As shown in Table 3, Swift Sampling consistently boosts UniComps accuracy across all retention ratios r, achieving peak gain of +1.6 points on MLVU (r = 0.15). These results demonstrate that Swift Sampling offers drop-in improvement for token compression pipelines. 7 Table 3: Swift Sampling for token compression All methods produce 32 frames as input to the video LLM. UniComp uses 32 uniformly sampled frames. Replacing uniform sampling with Taylor selection (shown as +Swift Sampling) (128 32 candidates) consistently improves the overall accuracy of UniComp [1] across all retain ratios r. Bold: best within block. All Method 10m 15m 30m # Tokens Vanilla 32f UniComp (r = 0.25) +Swift Sampling (Ours) UniComp (r = 0.20) +Swift Sampling (Ours) UniComp (r = 0.15) +Swift Sampling (Ours) UniComp (r = 0.10) +Swift Sampling (Ours) 6272 1568 1254 627 64.7 64.2 65.1 64.5 65.3 64.4 66.0 62.1 64. 61.4 60.0 60.6 61.0 60.4 59.8 61.8 59.6 60.8 54. 53.7 55.7 53.0 55.0 57.0 57.7 57.0 56.4 50.0 56.2 58. 56.2 58.3 62.5 56.2 64.6 62.

sections.title: 4.4 Application: Video Captioning
sections.content: Video captioning requires interpreting sequence of selected frames and generating coherent natural language description. We evaluate Swift Sampling on LLaVAOneVision [7] on the TempCompass benchmark [74] on the task of caption generation, where improved frame selection is expected to yield more informative captions. For evaluation, we follow the original protocol: we prompt GPT-4o (2024-11-20) [75] with the generated caption and ask it to answer corresponding multiple-choice question. correct answer indicates correct caption, and vice versa. As shown in Table 4, Swift Sampling improves captioning performance across nearly all categories, but struggles on attribute change. Table 4: Swift Sampling for Video Captioning improves accuracy across most categories in TempCompass [74].

4.5 Applications: Other Downstream tasks in Video-MME
sections.content: We analyze performance by task-category on Video-MME to isolate Swift Samplings core strengths. As seen from Table 5, Swift Sampling excels in reasoning-intensive tasks: Spatial Reasoning (+5.4%), Action Reasoning (+3.9%), Temporal Reasoning (+2.8%), and Action Recognition (+2.2%). We believe selecting frames that most distinctively capture the motion benefits these highlevel tasks. By contrast, performance regresses in tasks requiring global temporal continuity, such as Temporal Perception (3.6%) and Counting (2.2%) (qualitative examples in Appendix). We conjecture that these categories may demand uniform temporal coverage because even low-surprise regions of the video may carry taskrelevant information, requirement less suited to selective, surprise-based sampling.

sections.content: 53.7 55.0 74.8 37.7 73.4 61.9 55.1 65.8 59.3 78.6 63.6 40.7 57.5 (+3.9) 57.2 (+2.2) 71.2 (3.6) 35.4 (2.2) 74.3 (+0.9) 60.4 (1.4) 55.1 (+0.0) 66.1 (+0.3) 63.0 (+3.7) 83.9 (+5.4) 60.0 (3.6) 43.5 (+2.8) Below, we present thorough analysis of our method on LLaVA-OneVision [7]. Table 6: Effect of spatial aggregation. Patch grid for regionlevel pooling before computing the Taylor residual on MLVU. Global mean pooling provides the best balance across benchmarks. Bold: best. Choice of feature pooling. We study how the spatial granularity of feature aggregation affects frame selection quality. LLaVA-OneVision [7] uses SigLIP [13] as its vision encoder, which produces 14 14 token grid per frame. We first aggregate the per-frame token grid into an patch grid. Taylor residuals are computed for each grids mean feature, then averaged to produce single frame-level score. We sweep {1, 2, 4, 7, 14}, ranging from global mean pooling (S = 1, single region summarizing all 196 tokens) to no aggregation (S = 14, each region containing exactly one token). As shown in Table 6, global mean pooling (S = 1) achieves the best overall performance. We hypothesize that finer grids (e.g., = 14) dilute the temporal signal presumably because the local residuals could be dominated by texture noise and camera jitter. By contrast, the frame-level mean captures coherent scene-level transitions more relevant to frame selection. Given its superior robustness = 1 is our default aggregation strategy. Global mean (Ours) 2 2 4 4 7 7 14 14 (all tokens) 57.9 56.6 57.1 57.4 57.8 59.0 57.7 58.3 58.7 58.3 65.6 64.7 65.8 65.0 64.1 1 4 16

sections.content: 8 Table 8: Analyses on Taylor expansion order and frame budget. (a) Effect of Taylor expansion order on VQA accuracy across VideoMME, LongVideoBench (LVB), and MLVU. = 3 provides the best overall balance across benchmarks. (b) Impact of frame budget (K) on MLVU VQA accuracy. We compare uniform sampling and Cosine Uniqueness [1] against Swift Sampling. Results are reported for the full MLVU dataset and specific long-video subsets ( 10m, 15m, 30m). Gains over uniform sampling are indicated in the parentheses.

Choice of Feature Layer and Type. We study which layers and feature types yield the most predictable temporal dynamics, and thus, the most informative Taylor residuals for frame selection. We compare key features from the self-attention projection Wk against hidden features (encoder block outputs). As shown in Table 7, mean-pooled key features in the earliest layers of the vision encoder produce the lowest average residuals. We hypothesize that this is because early-layer features provide stable, low-level scene representations that evolve smoothly, making them highly predictable yet sensitive to sudden surprises. Deeper layers, though offer marginal gains, often prioritize holistic semantics over the motion dynamics required for precise temporal prediction. Thus, we use ℓ = 0 as our default choice. We present detailed visualization of all layers in Appendix. Key layer MLVU Video-MME LVB 1.02 1.05 1.07 1.09 ℓ = 0 ℓ = 1 ℓ = 2 ℓ = 3 57.9 56.3 57.1 57.7 65.6 64.3 65.8 65. 59.0 59.1 58.4 59.

sections.title: FLOPs
sections.content: Effect of Taylor Expansion Order. We investigate how the Taylor expansion order (as defined in Section 4.1) affects frame selection quality. As shown in Table 8a, VQA accuracy improves sharply from = 1 to = 3 across all three benchmarks, after which performance saturates. While Video-MME and LVB show no significant gains, MLVU exhibits marginal improvements at = 6. This suggests that low-order terms effectively capture the majority of predictable local dynamics and higher-order derivatives provide diminishing returns for identifying temporal surprises. Thus, we adopt = 3 as the default, striking balance between efficiency and predictive accuracy. Effect of Frame Budget. We evaluate the impact of the frame budget on VQA performance. From Table 8b, Swift Sampling consistently outperforms uniform sampling across all frame budgets, with significant gains on longer videos and under highly constrained budgets. For videos exceeding 30 minutes, Swift Sampling improves over uniform sampling by +12.5 points at = 4 and +10.4 points at = 2. Thus, as the frame budget tightens, identifying temporal surprises becomes critical for model reasoning and Swift Sampling offers computationally efficient solution for it.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion and Future Work
sections.content: We presented Swift Sampling, training-free framework for long-video understanding that identifies keyframes via Taylor residuals. By reusing only the early layers of VLMs vision encoder, our method adds negligible 0.02 overhead 30 less overhead than prior baselines. Swift Samplings lightweight design and consistent top-performance over prior training-free baselines makes it seamless drop-in for pipelines like token compression and captioning. Currently, Swift Sampling is query-agnostic to prioritize efficiency. This may occasionally lead it to select visually surprising but semantically irrelevant frames, such as shot transitions. Future work will explore adapting the Taylor signal to be task-sensitive. Extending the framework to audio and spatio-temporal modalities to achieve more holistic, context-aware understanding of video is another fruitful future direction.
